It began in the lines of the native infantry, spread very swiftly to the cavalry and then to... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. full_notes:
Rebels and the Raj 1857 is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 258 Late in the afternoon of 10 May 1857, the sepoys in the cantonment of Meerut broke out in mutiny.

The text explains that it began in the lines of the native infantry, spread very swiftly to the cavalry and then to the city.

The text explains that the ordinary people of the town and surrounding villages joined the sepoys.

The text explains that the sepoys captured the bell of arms where the arms and ammunition were kept and proceeded to attack white people, and to ransack and burn their bungalows and property.

The text explains that government buildings – the record office, jail, court, post office, treasury, etc. – were destroyed and plundered.

The text explains that as darkness descended, a group of sepoys rode off towards Delhi.

The text explains that the sepoys arrived at the gates of the Red Fort early in the morning on 11 May.

The text explains that it was the month of Ramzan, the Muslim holy month of prayer and fasting.

The text explains that the old Mughal emperor, Bahadur Shah, had just finished his prayers and meal before the sun rose and the fast began.

The text explains that the sepoys who had gathered under his window told him: “We have come from Meerut after killing all the Englishmen there, because they asked us to bite bullets that were coated with the fat of cows and pigs with our teeth.

The text explains that this has corrupted the faith of Hindus and Muslims alike.’’ Another group of sepoys also entered Delhi, and the ordinary people of the city joined them.

The text explains that europeans were killed in large numbers; the rich of Delhi were attacked and looted.

The text explains that it was clear that Delhi had gone out of British control.

The text explains that some sepoys rode into the Red Fort, without observing the elaborate court etiquette expected of them.

The text explains that they demanded that the emperor give them his blessings.

The text explains that surrounded by the sepoys, Bahadur Shah had no other option but to comply.

The text explains that they then attacked government buildings – the jail, treasury, telegraph office, record room, bungalows – burning all records.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 260 In the months of May and June, the British had no answer to the actions of the rebels.

The text explains that source 1 Ordinary life in extraordinary times What happened in the cities during the months of the revolt?

The text explains that the halalkhors (righteous) have become haramkhors (corrupt), many mohallas have not been able to earn for several days and if this situation continues then decay, death and disease will combine together to spoil the city’s air and an epidemic will spread all over the city and even to areas adjacent and around.

The text explains that 1.2 Lines of communication The reason for the similarity in the pattern of the revolt in different places lay partly in its planning and coordination.

The text explains that people were thus planning and talking about the rebellion. Ü Read the two reports and the descriptions of what was happening in Delhi provided in the chapter.

The text explains that the direction of the business is in able hands.” The tahsildar was later identified as the principal rebel leader of Bijnor.

The text explains that the pattern of the mutinies and the pieces of evidence that suggest some sort of planning and coordination raise certain crucial questions.

The text explains that it is difficult on the basis of the available documents to provide direct answers to such questions.

The text explains that source 2 REBELS AND THE RAJ Mutiny – a collective disobedience of rules and regulations within the armed forces Revolt – a rebellion of people against established authority and power.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 262 1.3 Leaders and followers To fight the British, leadership and organisation were required.

The text explains that in Jhansi, the rani was forced by the popular pressure around her to assume the leadership of the u p r i s i n g .

The text explains that many of the villagers were prosperous and saw the British land revenue system as oppressive: the revenue demand was high and its collection inflexible.

The text explains that shah Mal’s men attacked government buildings, destroyed the bridge over the river, and dug up metalled roads – partly to prevent government forces from coming into the area, and partly because bridges and roads were seen as symbols of British rule.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 264 1.4 Rumours and prophecies Rumours and prophecies played a part in moving people to action.

The text explains that the British tried to explain to the sepoys that this was not the case but the rumour that the new cartridges were greased with the fat of cows and pigs spread like wildfire across the sepoy lines of North India.

The text explains that captain Wright, commandant of the Rifle Instruction Depot, reported that in the third week of January 1857 a “low-caste” khalasi who worked in the magazine in Dum Dum had asked a Brahmin sepoy for a drink of water from his lota.

The text explains that there was the rumour that the British government had hatched a gigantic conspiracy to destroy the caste and religion of Hindus and Muslims.

The text explains that we cannot understand the power of rumours and prophecies in history by checking whether they are factually correct or not.

The text explains that as you know, from that time, under the leadership of Governor General Lord William Bentinck, the British adopted policies aimed at “reforming” Indian society by introducing Western education, Western ideas and Western institutions.

The text explains that with the cooperation of sections of Indian society they set up English-medium schools, colleges and universities which taught Western sciences and the liberal arts.

The text explains that on a variety of pleas, like misgovernment and the refusal to recognise adoption, the British annexed not only Awadh, but many other kingdoms and principalities like Jhansi and Satara.

The text explains that once these territories were annexed, the British introduced their own system of administration, their own laws and their own methods of land settlement and land revenue collection.

The text explains that it seemed to the people that all that they cherished and held sacred – from kings and socio-religious customs to patterns of landholding and revenue payment – was being destroyed and replaced by a system that was more impersonal, alien and oppressive.

The text explains that read the section once more and explain the similarities and differences you notice in the ways in which leaders emerged during the revolt.

The text explains that the takeover of Awadh in 1856 was expected to complete a process of territorial annexation that had begun with the conquest of Bengal almost a century earlier.

The text explains that the British government also wrongly assumed that Wajid Ali Shah was an unpopular ruler.

The text explains that (c) The ally would have to provide the resources for maintaining this contingent.

The text explains that resident was the designation of a representative of the Governor General who lived in a state which was not under direct British rule.

The text explains that source 3 Ü Read the entire section and discuss why people mourned the departure of Wajid Ali Shah.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 268 and issues, traditions and loyalties worked themselves out in the revolt of 1857.

The text explains that thus neither taluqdars nor peasants had any reasons to be happy with the annexation.

The text explains that now, under the British, the peasant was directly exposed to overassessment of revenue and inflexible methods of collection.

The text explains that by the 1850s there were other reasons for their discontent.

The text explains that refer to Maps 1 and 2 and plot the areas where the revolt was most widespread.

The text explains that how close or far were these areas from the colonial cities?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 2: Rebels and the Raj 1857
